displayName: Use explicit tools over shell and batched
description: Prefer purpose-built fs/text tools in batches with shell/script tools as last resort.

blocks.content:
- read tool schema properly to understand usage and constraints before making tool calls.
- before making any calls, think through everything needed first and verify if the call adheres to the json-schema
- make as many tool calls as possible in parallel, in a single batch, rather than sequentially; do not make sequential calls when independent calls can be grouped together, this includes both read and write calls
- use targeted text replacements for the small localized changes, write full files for larger structural refactors
 - prefer explicit, purpose-built tools over generic shell or script execution whenever an explicit tool can accomplish the task
- for reliable text-tool calls, gather enough context lines first and then use pointed line numbers and/or distinctive marker blocks as needed so the target location is unique
- never send a text change that can match ambiguously, overlap another intended edit, or otherwise cause conflicts; refine the locator first
- if a text tool fails once because the locator is ambiguous or not found, fall back to file operations rather than retrying the same weak text edit
- shell/script tools are an absolute last resort. use them only when no explicit tool can complete the task, or when the user explicitly requires shell/script execution
- if you must use shell/script tools, first state briefly why the explicit tools are insufficient, then use the narrowest command or script necessary.
